data.title: Lumber Prices And WOOD
data.description:
In a January 14, 2025, Seeking Alpha article on lumber and the WOOD ETF, I concluded:

WOOD’s twenty-year monthly chart shows that technical support is at the September 2022 $63.78 low, with resistance at the May 2021 $98.98 high. At around $76.30 per share on January 14, 2025, risk-reward dynamics suggest a nearly 1:2 ratio, favoring the upside. Moreover, the trend of higher lows and higher highs since the September 2022 support level points to a bullish trend in early 2025. I favor long positions in WOOD with stops below $62 per share. The odds favor another move higher in the lumber futures arena, supporting higher prices for the WOOD ETF product in 2025. The Los Angeles wildfires and the need to rebuild the major U.S. city will increase wood demand over the coming months and years.

Nearby CME lumber futures were at the $582 per 1,000 board feet level on January 14, 2025, and have moved slightly higher. Meanwhile, the WOOD ETF was $76.29 on January 14 and has declined. However, WOOD held above the $62 per share level, reaching a low of $66.25 on April 9 as tariff fears gripped stocks, lumber, and markets across all asset classes. Since then, WOOD has recovered, making scale-down buying an optimal strategy.

Lumber futures are slightly higher in 2025

The CME’s continuous physical lumber futures chart shows that the nearby contract closed 2024 at $580.50 per 1,000 board feet.

The monthly chart indicates that lumber futures reached a high of $699 in March 2025. The price at $618 in mid-June 2025 was higher than the closing level at the end of 2024.

Lumber remains a highly illiquid futures market, therefore, proxies such as the WOOD ETF and other lumber-related equities and ETFs that correlate with lumber prices over time and serve as alternatives to the futures arena.

The WOOD ETF owns international lumber-related companies

The top holdings of the iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF include:

At $72.19 per share, the WOOD ETF had $198.70 million in assets under management. WOOD trades an average of 3,635 shares daily and charges a management fee of 0.41%. WOOD’s $1.09 blended dividend translates to a 1.51% yield, which covers the expense ratio in a little over one quarter.

Tariffs have weighed on the WOOD ETF, which has underperformed lumber prices in 2025

The WOOD ETF closed 2024 at $76.34 per share. While lumber prices are higher than the 2024 closing level in mid-June 2025, the WOOD ETF was 5.44% lower at $72.19 per share.

That April 2, 2025, “Liberation Day” announcement in the United States caused markets across all asset classes to plunge.

The daily chart shows that the WOOD ETF product reached a $66.25 per share bottom before turning higher. The ETF held a critical technical support level on April 7, 2025.

The chart shows that WOOD’s April 7 low of $66.25 per share held above the critical technical support level of $63.78, which was established at the September 2022 low.

The bullish and bearish cases for lumber and WOOD

The bullish case for the WOOD ETF and lumber prices is:

  • The Fed’s favored inflation indicator, the personal expenditures price index, or PCE, increased only 0.1% in April, putting the annual inflation rate at 2.1%, close to the Fed’s 2% target.
  • Lower interest rates over the coming months could increase the demand for new home construction as mortgage rates decline.
  • Bullish price action in the stock market since the early April 2025 correction could push WOOD and other lumber-related equities higher.
  • While the monthly chart highlights a sideways trend in the WOOD ETF, lumber futures have made higher lows and higher highs over the past year.

The bearish case for the WOOD ETF and lumber prices is:

  • The U.S. debt, at nearly $37 trillion, could keep interest rates elevated.
  • Moody’s recently downgraded U.S. sovereign debt, which has put upward pressure on interest rates.
  • If interest rates remain higher for longer, it will be challenging for lumber and the WOOD ETF to rally. The Fed kept the short-term Fed Funds Rate unchanged at the June FOMC meeting.

The bottom line is that bullish and bearish factors are pulling lumber prices (and the WOOD ETF) in opposite directions in June 2025.

Raise WOOD stop levels to below $65 per share

Buying the WOOD ETF on price weakness since mid-January 2025 has been optimal, as the ETF dropped from $76.29 to $66.25 and recovered to over $74 per share. I suggested a stop on long positions below the $62 per share level in my mid-January article. In June 2025, the stop should now be below the $65 level, as the April 7 low of $66.25 is now the critical technical support level.

WOOD’s Seeking Alpha ETF grades reflect the recent price action with an F in momentum. The 0.41% management fee receives a B- in expenses, while the blended dividend earns a C grade. Volatile lumber prices validate the D+ in risk, while WOOD’s assets under management and daily volume earns a C- in liquidity.

Lumber futures are highly illiquid, which makes trading or investing in the futures arena dangerous. Meanwhile, the WOOD ETF is closely correlated with lumber prices and serves as an acceptable proxy for market participants seeking long exposure to lumber prices. The underperformance compared to lumber prices since the end of 2024 makes the WOOD ETF a buy at the current price level.

data.title: Ciena: Reasonable Valuation And Attractive Growth Outlook (Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Ciena Corporation (NYSE:CIEN) in Dec’24, which I downgraded to a hold rating as the valuation was no longer attractive, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am now upgrading the stock. The 2Q25 results confirmed that CIEN is entering a powerful new growth cycle. AI demand is no longer just a narrative; it’s showing up in the numbers. Cloud orders are doubling, new AI-specific use cases are emerging, and service provider demand is rebounding.

2Q25 Earnings Review

Total revenue grew ~24% y/y to $1.13 billion, at the high end of guidance. By segment, networking platforms grew 28.1% y/y to $866.3 million, driven by a 38.1% y/y growth in optical networking. The software segment also did very well, growing ~14% y/y with Blue Planet revenue up 93.6% y/y to $28 million. On the margin side, adj. gross margin fell 250 bps y/y to 41%, but this was mainly driven by a higher mix of new solutions, which carry lower initial margins. Higher incentive compensation linked to strong bookings also pushed up OPEX. While this margin pressure may raise concerns, I see it as front-loaded investments due to the strong demand. If anything, this validates the narrative that CIEN is benefiting from the optical upgrade cycle.

Cloud and AI Demand

2Q25 results validated that AI-driven network demand is a major catalyst for CIEN. The surge in demand must be very strong, so much so that management described the market as experiencing a “step function” in demand, which I infer as a sharp inflection and not just a mere recovery. We can see this playing out very clearly in the cloud provider segment, which saw 85% y/y growth in 2Q25, growing to a record $428 million (now accounts for 38% of total revenue). Remember, this segment grew 0% in 1Q25 and was down between 2-4Q24.

The impressive part here is the order momentum: cloud provider orders are on track to double in FY25 (per management). Notably, three of the company’s top five customers in 2Q25 were hyperscalers, which goes to show that CIEN is being seen as one of the go-to vendors in supporting massive AI workloads. More broadly, I think these figures have a very positive impact on the stock’s narrative, as AI-driven growth is no longer just a narrative in the air but is a real growth driver with tangible numbers to back it up.

Strategic Wins Confirm Expansion into New High-Value AI Applications

CIEN is not just gaining a lot of momentum from the secular tailwind; it is also further embedding itself into more parts of the value chain, and this adds more growth drivers to the equity story. CIEN announced two major design wins that demonstrate this movement, where it is moving beyond traditional data center interconnect [DCI].

The first is the deployment with a global cloud provider to connect GPU clusters that are distributed in different regions. Remember that I called this out previously, that hyperscalers are being forced to spread their AI training workloads across multiple sites, creating demand for extremely high-capacity optical connections over long distances. This is playing out as expected, and revenue from this win will begin flowing in late FY25 and is expected to scale into FY26. The broader implication is that CIEN has proven it is able to capture a larger share of the entire AI-infrastructure market.

The second win is an out-of-band management network inside the data center. This is less impactful than the first, but the idea is the same. This shows that CIEN expertise in optics can be used even for intra-facility use cases.

If we piece these developments together, what we have is that CIEN's total addressable market has expanded, and I believe this has significantly expanded the growth runway for the business.

Service Provider Recovery Solidifies as MOFN Activity Hits All-Time Record

While the momentum in the cloud is the main highlight for CIEN, the recovery in the service provider market is also becoming a powerful growth engine. In my last write-up, I noted that there were early signs of demand stabilization from this customer base. The 2Q25 results confirm that this trend is not only real but accelerating. Management described a clear “improving trend line,” particularly among Tier 1 North American carriers, where inventory normalization is now largely complete.

What makes this entire dynamic compelling and very positive is that it gives CIEN another avenue to tap into the booming AI-driven infrastructure buildouts. And what validated this is that CIEN has achieved an all-time record for MOFN (Managed Optical Fiber Network) activity in 1H25. This suggests that service providers are actively building out dedicated network capacity for cloud partners, which means CIEN is indirectly benefitting from AI-driven infrastructure buildouts even when not selling directly to hyperscalers.

Yeah, we're seeing a lot of MOFN activity in India. It's not translated to a massive amount of revenues yet, but we've taken the orders and it will. We're seeing MOFN activity throughout the globe. And I think it's just really -- it goes hand-in-hand with the whole cloud buildout. They can't build everywhere with their own network, and so it's augmented by these MOFN opportunities. We've seen that in North America as well. There's been some, as you know, large announcements around some of the larger carriers partnering there, with Lumen and Zayo, et cetera. We're seeing that reflected in pretty much all parts of the globe. Asia, outside of India, we're beginning to see that on -- in Europe. We've seen activity, it's the largest amount of activity that we've seen, and it really just goes hand-in-hand with the overall strategic plans for these cloud providers. 2Q25 call

Valuation

Thankfully, CIEN’s valuation has come down to a more palatable level. The stock used to trade at 30x forward PE (which I thought was too expensive), but at the current 25x, I see a plausible path towards 21% upside even if I assume multiples continue to contract. What changed in my current model is that I increased the revenue growth outlook to reflect the order momentum and recovery in the service providers market. But I kept my margin assumptions the same. This translates to a net income expectation of $632 million. Attaching 20x to it, which is the high end of CIEN’s historical trading range, I got a share price target of $89. The interesting thing here is multiples may not contract at all, since CIEN is now entering a strong growth cycle with numbers to back it up. Suppose the market continues to value CIEN at 25x; the upside is a lot more attractive than what I modeled.

Risk

While demand is strong, near-term margin could stay depressed for longer than expected if it continues to capture new deals. This may look optically negative and cause pressure on the stock. CIEN also has meaningful customer concentration, particularly among cloud and Tier 1 service providers. Any slowdown in spending from these groups would have a direct impact. Lastly, CIEN growth may face periods of lumpiness as AI infrastructure buildout demand can be lumpy, and this may cause CIEN to report periods of slow growth that may spook the market.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my recommendation for CIEN is an upgrade to buy. I like the demand visibility, improved positioning in this AI infrastructure space, and also tangible evidence of recovery in the service provider market. With valuation now at a cheaper level, I see a path towards >20% upside.

data.title: KBWR: High LTD Ratios, Weak Fundamentals Are Reasons To Avoid Small Regional Banks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

There aren't many compelling reasons to buy the Invesco KBW Regional Banking ETF (NASDAQ:KBWR). In this article, I'll discuss a concerning trend in loan-to-deposit ratios for small commercial banks and compare KBWR's fundamentals to those of four other banking ETFs, which reveals its flaws in terms of quality and growth factors. As a result, I don't see a good investment opportunity at the moment, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

KBWR Overview

Strategy Discussion

KBWR tracks the KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index, which, according to its methodology document, "is designed to track the performance of U.S. regional banks and thrifts that are publicly traded in the U.S." I've summarized the main points from that document below.

1. Eligible securities must be classified as engaged in regional banking activities as determined by the Index Committee and meet specific security type, liquidity, and float requirements.

2. At each rebalancing period, the Index employs a modified market capitalization weighting methodology, such that no securities exceed a 4% allocation and that no more than five securities reach that cap. After the Index applies this first weight cap, all remaining securities (i.e., those outside the top five) are capped at 2%.

3. The Index rebalances after the third Friday each March, June, September, and December.

Since the Index Committee has full discretion when applying the "regional bank" designation, this has enormous implications for what KBWR holds. For example, I found it excluded 90 stocks in the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE), whose holdings are guided by Global Industry Classification Standards, or GICS. Many of those exclusions are larger stocks, so while the Index follows a modified market cap weighting scheme, the holdings are mostly in the small-cap category.

Peer Group and Performance

In this article, I will compare KBWR with four ETFs, as follows:

  1. Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB)
  2. SPDR S&P Bank ETF (KBE)
  3. iShares U.S. Regional Banks ETF (IAT)
  4. SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE)

All ETFs have a 0.35% expense ratio except for IAT, which charges 0.40%. They're also all well established, with about a 14+ year track record, though KBWR's $46.50 million in assets under management is tiny by comparison.

Since its inception on November 1, 2011, KBWR has delivered a 233.89% return, in line with the two other regional bank ETFs, IAT and KRE. Meanwhile, KBWB and KBE, which also include diversified banks, outperformed with gains of 358.94% and 277.40%, respectively.

Comparing KBWR and KBWB is a good way to distinguish the returns between small regional banks and large U.S. national money centers. By design, the two do not overlap, so with KBWB, you will gain exposure to large banks and brokerages like JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Morgan Stanley (MS).

As shown below, these banks have regularly beaten their smaller counterparts on the rolling five-year returns chart. For the month ending May 31, 2025, KBWB was up by 2.37% per year (14.47% vs. 12.10%), and the gap almost reached 6% per year in October 2021.

KBWR Analysis

LTD Ratio Trends: Large vs. Small Banks

Over the last 15 years, the gap separating the average annual loan-to-deposit ratio for small and large domestically chartered U.S. commercial banks has steadily risen. As shown below, loans as a percentage of deposits were actually smaller for small banks by about 6%, but now, the average LTD ratio for small banks is 19% higher for the first five months of 2025.

As noted in this article published by the Philadelphia Federal Reserve from Q3 2017, "deposits...are a stable source of funding for banks...a bank that finds itself with too few deposits to fund loans must rely more heavily on non-deposit sources of funds, whose availability and price are much more sensitive to changing economic or financial conditions." The authors expressed concern that a rising LTD ratio for small banks could indicate comparatively higher liquidity risk and lower lending standards, and based on my extension of the analysis to 2025, the LTD ratio for small banks has consistently remained above 80%, except for a brief reprieve to the low 70s due to the COVID-19 pandemic. I suggest these persistently high LTD ratios are why investors have steered clear of the small banks held by KBWR, and why it's lagged KBWB substantially since its inception in 2011.

KBWR Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

KBWR isn't a leader on any key fundamental metric, either. As shown in the table below, which covers the fund's top 25 holdings (60.50% of the total), KBWR's profitability metrics are below average, its valuation ratios are only average, and its historical and forward-looking earnings per share growth are generally poor, especially compared to KBWB.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

KBWR: It Leans Small

Just to cover my earlier point about how KBWR has a small-cap lean, consider the difference between its weighted average market cap and KBWB's ($4.94 vs. $169.08 billion). Its market cap is also smaller than IAT and KRE, and as shown below, 74.95% of its holdings are assigned to the small-cap segment, based on the latest breakpoints used to divide the S&P 1500 Composite Index (less than $7.4 billion). That's 39.43% above the peer group average.

KBWR: Quality Metrics At/Below Average

KBWR's weighted average ROA is only 0.96%, which is the lowest of the five. The same goes for its 8.66% ROE figure, and except against KBWB, its 26.85% is also behind its peers. These metrics all feed into the fund's 5.01/10 profit score, which I derived from each security's Seeking Alpha Factor Grade. To be sure, neither fund's score is great, but IAT appears to be the highest-quality fund of the five at the moment.

KBWR: Growth Rates Lacking, Valuation Only Average

The fundamental analysis table shows KBWR's constituents have increased earnings per share by 2.53% per year over the last year, which includes 3.38% over the last year. In contrast, KBWB's 26.30% growth rate last year represented a massive turnaround, and it's no surprise that it outperformed KBWR by 23.96% last year and is up another 10.66% YTD through June 18.

Earnings growth is what's driving bank stock returns, and based on consensus one-year earnings per share estimates, the trend should continue in favor of KBWB (14.47% vs. 8.66%) and, to a lesser extent, KRE (10.09%). In my view, there's not a compelling valuation reason to pick KBWR - its 10.84x forward P/E ratio is close to average, and even though its 1.03x trailing P/B ratio is attractive, it's not enough to offset its growth deficit.

Investment Recommendation

After reviewing KBWR's performance, fundamentals, and LTD ratio trends for larger and small U.S. commercial banks, I don't believe it's a compelling buy. I'm concerned that LTD ratios are back above 80% for many of the small banks held by KBWR, and from an earnings growth perspective, its holdings also aren't bouncing back as strongly as their larger counterparts. I might pick KBWR over the more diversified KRE, but for now, my suggestion is to stick with the larger, liquid, and more profitable large banks. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to the discussion below.

data.title: Robinhood: All Good Things End With Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) has proven to be tariff-resistant as it continues to benefit from incredible growth in transaction revenues. The company has made a solid push into GAAP profitability and management has committed to a share repurchase program. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and the Gold membership is resonating with consumers. I fear that the current valuation has gotten ahead of itself, as it is pricing in many years of growth. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating until the fundamentals catch up with the stock price.

HOOD Stock Price

I last covered HOOD in February, where I upgraded the stock to a buy rating on account of my view that the company would take market share long term. The stock is up 23% since then.

I am backtracking my positive coverage as I fear that I may have incorrectly lowered my return hurdles amidst an elongated bullish run.

HOOD Stock Key Metrics

HOOD is a brokerage firm which is known for its popularity among more-speculative retail investors. That reputation might be undeserved given the company’s aggressive product velocity - I prefer to instead view it as a rare innovator in a historically innovation-barren brokerage industry. Many of its “innovations” tend to be easily replicable and eventually adopted by competitors, but being first can mean market share gains.

The latest quarter saw the company continue its rapid growth, with total platform assets jumping 70% YoY to $221 billion. I note that roughly $40 billion of assets were brought over from its acquisition of TradePMR.

The company’s Robinhood Gold product has continued to gain in popularity, as evidenced by the adoption rate rising to 12.4% in the quarter.

The company saw net revenues jump 50% YoY, powered by 77% YoY growth in transaction-based revenues.

That 77% growth rate came in large part due to a 100% jump in crypto transaction-based revenues, though equity and options transaction revenues grew rapidly as well. I note that the company is beginning to lap tough comparables, and transaction-based revenue growth rates should moderate within the next 2 to 3 quarters.

The company saw its adjusted EBITDA jump 90% YoY to $470 million, representing a 51% margin.

The company was GAAP profitable for the 6th consecutive quarter, though I caution that there appears to be some cyclicality in this business, with investors appearing to trade more frequently during bullish rallies.

HOOD ended the quarter with $4.6 billion in cash and investments versus no debt.

The company repurchased $322 million of stock in the quarter, marking a meaningful uplift from prior quarters but reasonable given the booming profits. It appears that one driver of the accelerated share repurchases was to offset the dilution from the TradePMR acquisition.

On the conference call, management noted that they had increased the authorization to a total of $1.5 billion with expectations to deploy $800 million over the next two years with “flexibility to accelerate if market conditions warrant.” Management also noted ambitions to eventually allow access to private markets, which would mark one of the bigger innovations in recent years.

Is HOOD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, the stock traded at around 19x sales. Consensus estimates call for a steep deceleration in top-line growth moving forward.

It is hard to say if these consensus estimates are too conservative or too optimistic. On the one hand, HOOD looks capable of taking market share for many years to come. On the other hand, the vast majority of its growth appears to be driven by a surge in transaction revenues, something that seems to be highly cyclical in nature. The company will also be lapping tough comparables on that increased engagement moving forward.

Even if we assume that the company can sustain around 16% growth for the next 5 years, the stock still looks richly valued. I assume that HOOD can sustain 34% net margins over the long term, which would put it in the same league as Charles Schwab (SCHW). This should be considered an aggressive assumption, given the heavy reliance on promotions and marketing in driving the growth rates here. That places the stock at around 55x long-term earnings, which would represent a 3.4x price to earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’) based on 16% projected growth. While such a high PEG ratio is not unheard of and does not necessarily scream “bubble,” I typically look for stocks in the 1x to 2x range as the lower valuation would allow for a greater chance of outperformance. I would be more interested in the stock if it traded under $45 per share, which would represent an 11x sales multiple and would still be at the higher end of the fair value range.

HOOD Stock Conclusion

HOOD has performed very well, but all good things end with valuation, as it is perhaps not so commonly said in the investing world. It might seem curious to be downgrading the stock even with strong revenue growth, torrid margin expansion, a net cash balance sheet, and ongoing share repurchase program, but the valuation looks quite stretched to me. It is anyone’s guess when the bullish rally in the broader market or HOOD come to an eventual end, but buying stocks at such aggressive valuations sets the stakes too high, especially as there remains many other attractive opportunities among high-quality growth stocks like those I have found for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am downgrading HOOD to a neutral rating.

data.title: Utz Brands: A Snack Stock To Watch, But Not To Bite Just Yet
data.description:
Thesis: Reasonably Valued, But Not Yet a Buy

Utz Brands, Inc (NYSE:UTZ) isn’t salty enough to chase just yet, but it’s a name worth keeping on your watchlist. Utz is trading at a 15.5x forward P/E multiple, slightly below the peer average of 16.5x. I believe Utz is fairly priced given its minimal revenue growth and modest forward EPS CAGR at 11% for the period 2025-2027. The stock is not yet attractive to buy, with a low dividend yield of 1.84% that lies below both the peer group and the sector median. Utz’s management has a plan under execution to increase its market penetration across the U.S., as reflected in the 1.6% revenue growth QoQ. In contrast, Utz has yet to improve its net margin, which stands below the sector median. Altogether, I’m rating UTZ a Hold, until we see improvements in the next quarters in both the top and bottom lines.

Utz Brands: 100 Years of Flavor, Focused on the Power Four

Utz Brands is an established American snack food company, founded in 1921, based in Hanover, Pennsylvania. Utz operates and sells its products in the U.S. Utz manufactures a wide portfolio of snack products, primarily focusing on potato chips, pretzels, cheese snacks, and pork rinds.

Utz owns a number of flagship brands in the snack food category, with a focus on what it calls the “Power Four Brands”, which are Utz, Zapp’s, On the Border Chips & Dips, and Golden Flake. These brands are considered the core growth engine for the company.

Utz currently operates in 20 states, which represent its core market, and accounts for 56% of its net sales, with a 6.5% market share of retail sales, as noted in the Q1 presentation. Utz’s management plans to expand in the other 30 states, in which it holds a 3% market share, as of Q1. It’s notable that its retail sales increased at a CAGR of 7.2%, which is impressive, given its competition with major players in the salted snacks industry, like Frito-Lay, which dominates the majority of the market.

Utz Grows Volume, Not Margins: A Clean-Label Play With Long-Term Potential

Currently, Utz is expanding its portfolio through innovative products, especially in its better-for-you snacks segment. In 2025, Utz added new products to the Boulder Canyon and On The Border brands, like tortilla chips and ridged potato chips cooked in avocado oil and GMO-free.

I think focusing on niche segments like clean labels through its brands Boulder Canyon and On The Border, would be more effective for driving Utz’s growth rather than relying on basic salted snacks, which is a mature highly competitive market.

On a quarterly basis, revenue grew by 1.6%, driven by 4.9% organic net sales growth in branded salty snacks. This growth was mainly led by Boulder Canyon, as noted in the presentation. The surge in Boulder Canyon sales implies that Utz is gaining traction in the clean-label snack segment.

Revenue growth was slightly offset by a 3.4% decline in net price realization. The reason behind this is that Utz made offers and discounts to increase first-time purchases, especially in the expansion states, according to the earnings call.

Utz’s branded snacks segment contributed 87% of total sales, while non-branded snacks accounted for 13%. This represents a decline from Q1 2024 when non-branded snacks contributed 18% to revenue. The dip is attributed to a decline in partner brands and private label business, in addition to the disposition of assets, according to management.

I speculate that this move reflects Utz’s pivot toward focusing on brand strength in order to gain more market share and enhance its pricing power.

Given that the private label space not only carries lower margins than branded products but also exposes Utz to competition with larger private label players, who could offer better prices than Utz.

As shown in the table below, gross profit declined modestly by 1.17%. The decline was attributed to increased promotional spending and investment in capacity and distribution, according to the 10-Q. It may seem unusual to see gross profit margin decline, despite volume growth. However, Utz’s decision to offer packs with more volume at the same price was necessary to gain more market share in expansion states, in addition to investing in its capacity. Given the fact that it still holds just an average of 4.75% market share nationwide, which implies significant room for growth.

GAAP net profit grew in dollars by 137%. However, This figure can be misleading because Utz faced a distorted Q1 2024. Its net profit was disrupted by the sale of assets and higher income taxes. Additionally, a non-recurring loss warrants liability. Hence, it will be more accurate to compare its profitability to the sector median rather than its historical performance. Utz’s trailing twelve-month net income margin at around 2% is well below the sector median of roughly 4%. However, I think expanding in clean label space and more concentration on branded products could improve its profitability margins upward in the long run.

To sum up, Utz showed mixed signals, evidenced by revenue growth but compressed margin. However, I don’t see the margins' compression as a red flag, it’s more likely a sacrifice by management of short-term profitability for long-term growth, considering that Utz has significant room to grow its market share.

Metric

Q1 2024

Change

Branded Focus and Cost Discipline Power Utz’s Positive EPS Trajectory

The consensus revenue estimate for the full year is around $1.42B, which represents a year-over-year growth of 1.5%. It’s worth noting that Utz’s revenue in FY 2024 declined YoY because of the divestiture of its Good Health and R.W. Garcia brands, which lowered its sales volume. However, this move also shows Utz’s strategy to pivot toward high-growth segments and focus on its core brands.

According to SA analysts’ estimates, earnings per share are expected to continue on their growth trajectory. Management also guided toward 10%-15% adjusted EPS growth.

The growing EPS is most likely to be attributed to continued sales momentum driven by Power Four Brands, especially Boulder Canyon's continuously expanding SKUs.

Furthermore, Utz aims to deliver $150MM in productivity savings through 2026.

Management initiated the saving program in 2024, and this was reflected in Q1 results, evidenced by a 100 basis point improvement in adjusted gross margin, which helped partially offset high promotional spending. The program could help offset the impact of management’s plan to invest in distribution and increase its capacity, as stated in the earnings call. Altogether, the future outlook looks good with a CAGR EPS growth of 11.7% for the period 2025-2027, as shown in the chart below. Market share growth also will support its growth trajectory.

Relative Valuation Analysis: Room for Growth or Fairly Priced?

As presented in the table below, UTZ appears to be priced slightly below the peer average, implying a possible undervaluation. From a forward P/E perspective, the market is pricing Utz below JJSF and VITL. This could be attributed to a healthier net margin of 4.79% and a broader product mix for JJSF.

I had the chance to cover VITL previously, so I don’t think UTZ is going to be rated anywhere close to VITL’s P/E ratio, considering that VITL has a higher growth potential and an outstanding net margin of 8.26%.

At the low end of the peer group are KLG, Dole, and NOMD. To start off with KLG, its EPS forward growth is nothing to compare with Utz. Given that Utz still has significant room for growth, unlike KLG which is already well-penetrated in the U.S. cereal market.

DOLE is trading at a cheaper price-to-earnings compared to Utz, due to DOLE’s weak profitability margins, with a gross margin of 8%, well below the sector median. Its net margin of 1.1% is also weaker than Utz’s 2%.

NOMD, a stock that I have also covered before, is trading at a discount because of its high exposure in the European market and the level of maturity it has achieved, which hinders its growth.

It’s understandable to see Utz offering a low dividend yield compared to its peers, given that it reinvests earnings to support its growth.
