Valuation Summary

I believe that Utz is fairly traded at the moment compared to its peers based on forward P/E.

Its premium P/E relative to its low-end peers reflects Utz’s growth potential.

The discount to high-end peers like VITL is coming from the fact that Utz is not going toward exceptional growth, with a modest forward revenue growth of 1.5%, which is slightly below the 1.7% sector median.

Additionally, Utz is positioned to invest heavily in its distribution network and capacity to support its expansion. Utz carries a high debt of around $1B. This is understandable in order to finance its growth, though it poses a risk of high-interest expense that could hinder its forward EPS growth.

In conclusion, I’m rating Utz a Hold.

The bullish case is to see better net margin, accompanied by high revenue growth above the sector's median that exceeds analysts’ expectations.

The bearish case involves slow or no revenue growth, which would signal a failed strategy to gain additional market share.

data.title: Hammond Power Solutions: High Voltage Fundamentals, Low Voltage Multiple
data.description:
Introduction

Hammond Power Solutions (TSX: TSX:HPS.A:CA / OTCPK: OTCPK:HMDPF) isn’t going to show up in anyone’s “next AI winner” screen. They make dry-type transformers and power-quality gear — old-school electrical hardware that keeps modern systems from frying. It’s not glamorous, but it’s hard to electrify anything at scale without this kind of equipment quietly working in the background. And right now, there’s more demand than supply across the board — EV charging, data centers, grid upgrades, microgrids — pick your secular trend.

Despite that setup, the stock still trades at ~15x earnings and under 10x EBITDA, with a clean balance sheet, record backlog, and new capacity just starting to come online. Execution has been consistent, earnings are climbing, and the margin profile is better than most peers — many of which trade at materially higher multiples. There are some working capital swings and India’s soft, but nothing thesis-breaking. At this valuation, with FY2026 earnings likely above $8/share, the upside looks good. I’m rating HPS a Buy.

Business Operations and Market Conditions

Hammond Power is the largest dry-type transformer maker in North America. These are air-cooled units — no oil — which makes them ideal for indoor or sensitive environments like server farms, battery storage, EV fast-charging, and renewables. Over half of what they ship is custom, and the scale isn’t trivial: more than 450,000 transformers and 9,000 SKUs go out the door each year.

The company also sells cast resin variants and a line of power quality components — mostly filters, reactors, and voltage suppression systems that help stabilize industrial equipment. That part of the business took a step back in 2024 thanks to project delays, but the product line was expanded, and production was shifted down to the new Mexico plant to support better margins when volumes recover.

By geography, about 68% of sales come from the U.S. and Mexico, 27% from Canada, and 5% from India. Canada grew the fastest in 2024, but India was down due to timing issues and election-related slowdowns. They’re not relying on India for growth at this point, but longer-term it probably matters.

Customers break out into three channels — distributors (69%), OEMs (20%), and private label (11%). That’s a nice spread in theory, though in practice, distributor and project business can blur when products are semi-custom and tied to specific timelines. Custom orders held up well even as standard stock softened late in 2024. Backlog jumped 17.7% sequentially in Q1 2025, which is probably the most telling data point in the whole setup.

To meet that demand, Hammond’s investing around C$80 million over three years. The Monterrey plant is the centerpiece — small transformer and power-quality capacity aimed mostly at the U.S. — and they’ve added warehouse space in Dallas. Lead times should come down, freight costs drop, and fulfillment gets easier. Assuming it works.

They’re not a startup, but they’re not a global giant either. With ~23% share of the North American dry-type market, Hammond sits between regional independents and the multinational behemoths like ABB and Siemens. They win with customization, speed, and relationships — not price. It’s not a wide moat, but it’s defensible.

Financials

FY2024 was solid. Revenue grew 11.0% to C$788.3 million, gross margins ticked up to 32.8%, and earnings from operations rose almost 14% to C$98.8 million. Net income came in at C$71.5 million (C$6.01/share), up 12.8%. Adjusted EBITDA was C$130.5 million — 16.5% margin. Nothing in there is a surprise, but it’s clean and directionally strong.

Metric FY2024 FY2023 YoY Change
Revenue C$788.3 million C$710.1 million +11.0%
Gross Profit C$258.6 million C$230.8 million +12.0%
Gross Margin 32.8% 32.5% +30 bps
Adjusted EBITDA C$130.5 million C$117.2 million +11.3%
Net Income C$71.5 million C$63.4 million +12.8%
Backlog (FYE) C$174.7 million C$174.9 million -0.1%

Q1 2025 held the line. Revenue was up 5.6% YoY to C$201.4 million, helped by U.S. (+7.8%) and Canada (+6.9%) — more than offsetting a steep 23.9% drop in India. Net income was C$26.2 million (C$2.20/share), and adjusted EBITDA was C$30.9 million — 15.4% margin, down a bit but still fine. Gross margin held at 31.5%. Some mix pressure and heavier opex, but nothing alarming.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Revenue C$201.4 million C$190.7 million +5.6%
Gross Profit C$63.5 million C$60.4 million +5.1%
Gross Margin 31.5% 31.7% -20 bps
Adjusted EBITDA C$30.9 million C$31.0 million -0.3%
Net Income C$26.2 million C$8.0 million +227.5%
Backlog (Q1-end) C$205.8 million C$173.2 million +18.8%

Backlog at the end of Q1 hit C$205.8 million — up 17.7% sequentially and 18.8% YoY. That’s the highest level they’ve ever reported, and it’s not fluff. Most of it’s tied to utility, infrastructure, and data center demand — all long-cycle, which helps buffer the weaker industrial orders.

Cash Flow and Capex

FY2024 cash flow looked good — C$64.8 million from operations, which easily covered C$40.6 million in capex and C$11.6 million in dividends. The balance sheet stayed clean.

Q1 2025 was more complicated. Operating cash flow swung to negative C$(3.0) million, mostly due to a C$30.4 million working capital draw. Capex was C$7.9 million. So total free cash outflow was C$(10.9) million. Not great, but also not a surprise given where they are in the expansion cycle. Inventories were built up for long-lead projects, and receivables jumped — probably timing-related, but worth watching.

They drew down more on their credit line — C$28 million outstanding versus C$13 million at year-end — but still held C$32.8 million in cash. That leaves them with ~C$4.8 million net cash and over C$100 million in unused availability. So they’re fine on liquidity, just not in accumulation mode right now.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Cash and Cash Equivalents C$32.8 million C$34.1 million -3.8%
Bank Operating Lines Drawn C$28.0 million C$13.0 million +115.4%
Net Cash (ex-leases) C$4.8 million C$21.1 million -77.3%
Inventory C$155.4 million C$137.6 million +12.9%
Accounts Receivable C$147.8 million C$138.1 million +7.0%

Management expects working capital to normalize once Monterrey ramps and fulfillment starts flowing through Dallas. If they get back to a C$25–30 million free cash flow run-rate by FY2026, none of this matters much. But that’s still an “if” until we see how the execution plays out.

Valuation

HPS trades at 15.05x trailing P/E, 9.87x EV/EBITDA, and 1.69x sales — all modest, especially for a company with no debt and 11% revenue growth. Industrial comps trade higher across the board — median sector P/E is 23.35x, EV/EBITDA at 13.1x. Even on forward estimates, Hammond screens attractively.

Company P/S P/E EV/EBITDA Revenue Growth (YoY) Net Income Growth (YoY)
Hammond Power Solutions 1.69 15.05 9.87 11% 12.8%
Pioneer Power Solutions (PPSI) 1.14 26.56 14.31 105.8% N/A (Turned Profitable)
Powell Industries (POWL) 2.03 12.71 8.35 44.8% 174.8%
Indo Tech Transformers (INDOTECH.NS) 3.53 33.80 22.34 35.8% 82.3%
Bharat Bijlee (BBL.NS) 1.75 24.90 14.01 32% 57.9%
Voltamp Transformers (VOLTAMP.NS) 4.55 27.03 19.12 19.7% 5.9%
Shilchar Technologies (SHILCTECH.BO) 9.82 41.66 30.17 41.6% 113%
TRANS & RECTI (TARIL.NS) 68.11 68.11 42.74 56% 360.4%
DAIHEN Corporation (6622.T) 0.69 12.97 7.59 20% -27.5%

Peer comps vary. Powell Industries is cheaper on EV/EBITDA (8.35x), but their business is more lumpy — bigger projects, broader scope. Indian peers like Voltamp, Shilchar, and Indo Tech all trade at much higher multiples — 19–30x EBITDA — which reflects both higher returns and a more speculative domestic market. Even Bharat Bijlee and Pioneer Power trade above Hammond on EV/EBITDA, despite arguably lower-quality earnings.

Hammond’s top-line growth (+11%) and net income growth (+12.8%) are better than most U.S. or Canadian industrials. And yet, valuation still lags — probably because of size, liquidity, Canadian listing, and perceived cyclicality. That said, the business is less tied to public utility capex cycles than some larger players, and it’s basically unlevered.

With Q1 already showing C$2.20 in EPS, a full-year figure closer to C$8.00 seems reasonable. At 14x, that’s a C$112 stock. Right now, it trades well below that. If margins expand with volume, a move to 18–20x earnings or 11–12x EBITDA wouldn’t be crazy. But the market probably waits for more proof before rerating.

Catalysts

A few things could help move the stock higher in the coming year. First, the Monterrey factory will be critical. Once it begins shipping power-quality filters and smaller transformers in late 2025, the plant should improve both delivery times and margins, particularly for U.S. orders. Domestic production also aligns well with U.S. nearshoring and Buy America trends.

Second, the product mix continues to shift in the right direction. Mesta filter lines are expanding, new frame sizes have been introduced, and demand from EV charging infrastructure and data centers remains strong. These higher-value segments should boost average selling prices and margin contribution over time.

Third, operating leverage is starting to show. FY2024 saw gross margin improvement despite flat power-quality sales and input inflation. If volume continues to grow and overhead is spread across more output, there’s room for additional gains — especially as the new capacity ramps up.

Finally, the 17.7% sequential backlog increase in Q1 signals strong demand. Custom project backlogs, which stretch across multiple quarters, help provide visibility and reduce reliance on short-cycle demand. That kind of backlog is rare in a micro-cap industrial, and could smooth revenue and earnings into FY2026.

Risks

Still, there are things that could go wrong. End-market demand is cyclical — construction, industrial capex, utilities, mining. If one or more slows down meaningfully, that would show up in order intake. Standard transformer sales already softened in late 2024.

Input costs are another issue. Copper, aluminum, and electrical steel remain volatile. HPS has done a good job passing along price increases, but there’s always a lag, and margin pressure is a risk in tighter environments.

The Monterrey plant is a swing factor. If it runs well, great. If there are delays, cost overruns, or integration issues, that could hurt both earnings and perception. Same goes for Mesta. Power-quality is a growth lever, but 2024 was a down year and it hasn’t rebounded yet.

India’s a weak spot. Q1 sales were down 23.9%, and while some of that’s elections and project delays, it’s not clear when or how that business regains momentum. Not critical yet, but disappointing.

Receivables have also been growing faster than revenue. Up 7% sequentially and 20% YoY. Could just be project billing timing, but worth keeping an eye on — especially if more revenue is tied to large custom systems.

Conclusion

Hammond Power’s not flashy, but it is delivering. Backlog’s at a high, Q1 came in clean, and the company’s doing what it said it would — building capacity, holding margins, and staying out of trouble. There’s no long-term debt, they’re funding everything internally, and gross margins are near cycle highs even with mix headwinds.

If Monterrey ramps as planned and power-quality recovers, earnings should move higher, and so should the multiple. At 18–20x forward earnings — which is hardly aggressive for a capital-light electrical name — the stock would be worth somewhere in the C$110–120 range. That’s ~40–50% upside without needing heroics. I rate HPS a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Progressive Turns Margin Strength Into Market Share Dominance
data.description:
When I opened my first position in Progressive (NYSE:PGR) years ago, it was a relatively good choice: an insurer with a good strategy and strong management. It has since become one of my best performing stocks. Progressive proved its strength during inflation, storms and market turmoil. While rivals were still recovering, it gained market share. The company posted the strongest monthly growth in auto policies in its history. That's remarkable for an insurer that already generates billions in premium income. For investors, it begs the question: is there more room for growth? My answer is yes. I expect the stock to rise 30% by 2027, driven by policy growth, pricing discipline and technology.

Rapid Growth Through Smart Timing

Progressive is now the second-largest US auto insurer. In Q1, its policy count grew 18%, the fastest in years. Progressive adjusted premiums early during the inflation spike. Competitors like Allstate, Travelers and GEICO followed only later. Those timing differences have given Progressive a structural advantage. Bank of America, which downgraded the stock in April 2025, pivoted shortly afterward due to explosive customer growth in March. Analysts called it a premium wave "the likes of which haven't been seen in 50 years."

Data and Speed as a Competitive Weapon

What sets Progressive apart is its obsession with data and speed. While others were still upgrading their systems, Progressive had real time pricing models up and running. In 2025, that's not a nice to have, it's a necessity: rates, claims frequency and parts prices are constantly changing due to geopolitics and scarcity. CEO Tricia Griffith said her team runs daily import tariff scenarios since late 2024 and can adjust pricing within weeks. In an industry where state regulation can take months, that is a competitive weapon.

The competition is stirring again. Analysts recently pointed to signs of increasing price pressure, especially in auto. But Progressive does not seem to be impressed. The company increased its marketing budget and continues to add policies, partly thanks to bundle benefits. The management emphasized that growth is a priority again now that core margins are healthy. The aggressive stance combined with scale means that competitors are struggling to keep up without sacrificing their margins.

Cautious Expansion in Home Insurance

Progressive is also growing in home insurance, but cautiously. In hurricane prone states such as Florida and California, the company is actually scaling down. Growth is mainly coming from less volatile states and from renters insurance. While others pull back, Progressive gains ground by targeting customers who also bundle their auto insurance. That approach is sensible: controlled growth with a focus on profitability, rather than reckless expansion.

Financial Performance and Outlook Are Exceptionally Strong

The Q1 2025 figures leave little to the imagination. Premiums rose 20% YoY. Net income reached $2.6 billion. The 86% combined ratio shows strong operational control. Even in April, when the US was hit by several storms, the ratio remained below 85%. The claims ratio improved due to lower accident frequency and favorable reserve estimates. Only higher acquisition costs (mainly advertising) pushed the combined ratio up slightly, showing that Progressive is consciously using part of its margin for further growth.

The investment result was also positive. Due to higher interest rates, interest income increased by 32% compared to last year. This significantly strengthens profitability. In addition, equity increased by almost $4 billion, partly due to increased value in fixed-income investments. The balance sheet remains strong, with a debt ratio of only 19%. This allows the company to continue to invest in growth or absorb setbacks without jeopardizing its dividend. Looking ahead, management indicates that most premiums are now at a level to cover costs. However, they continue to implement rate increases in certain segments, particularly home and commercial auto. The rate of premium growth will slow, but that mainly means that the big correction cycle is behind us. I expect the combined ratio to hold near 86% and EPS to reach $16 per share in 2025. If the rate of policy growth continues and margins remain stable, that is a realistic scenario.

Dividends and Valuation

Progressive pays a modest $0.10 quarterly dividend, plus a variable annual payout based on full year earnings. For 2024, that extra dividend amounted to $4.50 per share. Together with the fixed portion, this brought the total payout to $4.60 (a dividend yield of 1.7%). In lean years, this is lower, but that is what makes the policy flexible and sustainable. Progressive pays what it can but does not promise anything it cannot deliver. At less than $250 million per year, the fixed dividend is more than covered by quarterly profit alone. This gives investors certainty, while the company retains room to invest in growth or buybacks when attractive.

In terms of valuation, the stock is not cheap. Progressive trades at about 17x forward earnings, a premium to peers like Allstate or Travelers at 11-13x. The multiple is also substantial based on book value. However, the higher profit growth, better margins and robust balance sheet justify this premium. Progressive has been a quality player for years with above average return on equity. The company has consistently managed to create value throughout economic cycles. That makes the stock attractive for those who look a little further ahead.

Conclusion

Progressive is still a buy for me. I rate it "Buy" for investors with a 1-3 year or longer horizon. Strong margins, customer growth and a solid balance sheet make Progressive a strong growth name in the defensive sector. The share price has risen sharply since I bought it but I believe there is still 25-30% upside potential towards 2027, provided market conditions do not deteriorate dramatically.

However the risks are certainly there. If interest rates fall sharply, investment returns will decline. At the same time, lower interest rates would make financing for growth cheaper. Competition could also increase especially now that other players have their prices in line and are spending more on marketing. However, in my view, Progressive remains better positioned to fight that battle, thanks to its efficient organization and predictive pricing models. Another risk is extreme weather damage, but the company has proven to be able to withstand such events well without structural damage to profitability. The main difference with many other insurers is that Progressive continues to act forward. Progressive appeals to both cautious investors and those seeking long term growth from a well-run insurer. Therefore I remain long.

data.title: ETG: A Solid Choice For Retired Investors That Need Income
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Income Fund (NYSE:ETG), I issued a buy rating and discussed the efficiency of its underlying strategy and strength of the consistent distributions. Since the time of my prior coverage, the total return has exceeded that of the S&P 500 due to the combination of the market's quick recovery and the elevated distribution rate. I wanted to revisit ETG to specifically talk about why I believe that it can be a great option for retired investors that are looking to live off the distribution income their portfolio produces. When you are approaching the retired phase of your life, you need funds that demonstrate several traits of stability, such as:

  • A higher dividend yield to help cover lifestyle expenses.
  • Consistent dividend history that avoids cuts.
  • Tax-efficient if possible.
  • Lower volatility in price movement.
  • Exposure to a diversified range of sectors and holdings.
  • Price / NAV resilience over a long period of time.

I believe that ETG checks off most of these boxes and could make a compelling selection for retirees. Looking at the performance over the last five years, we can see that ETG's price has increased by about 38.2%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps to nearly 105% over the same time frame. One of the most appealing aspects of ETG is the generous starting dividend yield of 7.7% and the monthly payouts. The payouts have remained fairly consistent over a period of time, which makes it great for investors that want reliable income.

Despite the price recently increasing, I still believe that ETG offers an attractive value proposition. The fund provides a diverse global exposure to some of the highest quality large-cap companies in the world. ETG has an inception dating back to 2004 and has a management fee of about 1.05%. ETG utilizes an option strategy against its holdings as a way to generate additional income. However, there are some tradeoffs here with the inclusion of an option strategy that investors should take into consideration. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that the fund implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, ETG has total investments of $1.9B that is spread across 121 different holdings. The fund is most heavily focused on Financial companies, which make up 31.87% of total assets. This is followed by exposure to Information Technology and Industrials companies, accounting for 15.52% and 12.12% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up nearly 60% of total assets, so we can assume that these sectors will have the greatest impact on ETG's daily price movements and total return potential. The fund also provides global exposure, with a large portion of its equities being allocated to holdings outside of the US.

  • US Equities: 39.44% of assets
  • Foreign Equity: 47.18% of assets
  • Fixed Income/Preferred Stock: 12.73% of assets

I like the fact that ETG has exposure to technology companies because it better allows the fund to participate in capital appreciation throughout bull markets. ETG implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option writing strategy against its underlying holdings and volatility within the technology sector can help generate higher premiums. The higher the implied volatility, the higher the premiums that are received for writing options.

Looking at the top ten holdings, we can see many familiar large-cap names. Microsoft (MSFT) is the largest holding, making up 3.06% of assets. This is followed by exposure to other large-cap companies like Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG), and Apple (AAPL) to name a few. Although the fund does have exposure to these rapid-growth companies, ETG will likely underperform its own holdings from a price perspective. This is because the option strategy effectively caps the upside growth to whatever the selected strike price is on the written option.

Just to demonstrate this concept, I pulled a quick total return comparison of ETG against some of its top holdings over the last three years. We can see that ETG underperforms from a price perspective. Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that ETG's main focus is on income generation and the fund should not be utilized by investors that want to prioritize total return.

Valuation & Capital Preservation

Since ETG operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. Therefore, we can see below that ETG currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 4.1%. However, we can see that the discount level has become much smaller over the last year due to ETG's rising price. For instance, ETG has traded at an average discount to NAV closer to 8.3% over the last three-year period. Looking over the last ten-year history below, we can see that ETG has consistently traded in the discount territory. However, the fund has demonstrated unique qualities that would justify it trading in the premium to NAV territory.

For instance, ETG has a long track record of preserving investor capital over a long period of time. Going back more than two decades to its inception, ETG's price has remained nearly flat with the same price at its launch. This means that ETG has been able to effectively recover from declines driven by recessions, the Great Financial Crisis, and even the pandemic related crash back in 2020. Despite the limited upside potential due to the inclusion of options, the price has always been able to recover and this reinforces the idea that the fund's strategy is effective and management is efficient.

This capital preservation is what makes ETG such a great choice for retired investors. We can see that with the inclusion of all dividends paid out to shareholders, the total return since inception is 467.3%. So this means that if a retiree invested $100k into ETG at inception, their initially invested capital would still be in place, and they would have received more than 4x the amount back in dividends.

  • Initial Investment At Inception: $100k
  • Ending Balance: ~$100,750
  • Dividends Collected: ~$466,550

Since the fund also provides some global exposure, ETG has the ability to better navigate volatility experienced within US markets. Just as a recent example, markets declined throughout March and April due to uncertainty around President Trump's tariff proposals. While indexes like the S&P 500 have started to recover, ETG is currently outperforming by a wide margin of excess. Looking at the chart below, we can see how ETG outperforms SPFR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) in both price and total return, including all dividends.

Risk

While ETG may have brief periods of outperform against more traditional index-tracking ETFs, this isn't always the case when comparing performance over a longer time frame. For instance, if we look at the performance of ETG since its inception, it underperforms SPY. As we can see below, ETG's total since inception gets significantly outpaced over the last two decades. So if you are a younger investor, and you don't specifically have a need for income at the moment, you are likely doing yourself a disservice by choosing ETG over any traditional index-tracking ETF. While ETG's option strategy has still allowed the fund to participate in bull markets to an extent, the upside is still ultimately capped.

Additionally, ETG can experience more dramatic downdraws when markets are declining. ETG relies on a combination of net realized capital gains and net investment income to fuel the distributions that it pays. In other words, ETG will sell off positions that are at a gain and will also use any dividends or interest collected from positions to support the distributions it pays. As you can imagine, it can be difficult to capture positive realized capital gains when markets are declining. Therefore, the fund can find itself in a position where it's paying out distributions that are greater than the earnings it generated.

For instance, markets declined throughout 2022 in response to the Fed hiking interest rates and record-breaking inflationary data. SPY saw its price decline by about 19.5%, while ETG's price decline was much more severe at nearly 33.6%. This is because ETG didn't actually generate the necessary earnings to support the distribution, so it was pulling distributions straight from the fund's NAV.

Markets are still trying to assess the full impact of President Trump's tariff proposals and how it will affect sectors. Inflationary data remains higher than anticipated and the outlook around interest rate cuts is still questionable. There are many factors that have the potential to cause markets to decline over the next twelve months, so this heightened level of uncertainty can be a source of risk in the short term.

Dividend Income Consistency

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.1293 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.7%. This higher yield enables long-term investors to create a sizable stream of supplemental dividend income. During retirement, it's important that your income streams remain consistent over time, since your livelihood depends on it. You aren't actively earning an income from a job anymore, so it's better to stick with assets that aren't constantly reducing or adjusting your dividend income stream.

Looking over the last ten years, ETG has kept its payouts fairly steady. The distribution was previously raised from $0.1001 per share, up to the current payout, back in April 2024. Following the increase, I believe that the fund will be able to consistently out earn its distribution amount comfortably. If we take the current monthly payout and multiply it by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.5516 per share. The latest annual report for 2024 indicates that the fund has no problem out earning this amount during positive market conditions. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: ($0.10) per share
  • 2021: $7.76 per share
  • 2022: ($5.04) per share
  • 2023: $1.99 per share
  • 2024: $5.13 per share.

As you can see, ETG has generated excess earnings in three of the last five years. The pandemic happening in 2020 and markets saw a double-digit decline in 2022, so the performance here is just a result of macroeconomic conditions. However, there are periods where ETG has generated several years worth of distributions, such as in 2024. $5.13 per share in earnings means that ETG can effectively cover it annual distribution amount for more than three years. Therefore, I do not believe the fund has a reason to cut distributions at this time.

Even if the fund never formally raises dividend payouts, the consistency has enabled long-term investors to essentially create their own dividend growth. By simply reinvesting portions of the dividends received, investors would have been able to see their annual income amounts grow. The graph below assumes an initial investment of $25k at the start of 2016. The calculation assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $25k, but it does assume that all dividends received were reinvested so that more shares of ETG could be accumulated. The end result is that an investor would have been able to double their annual dividend income.

2016 Dividend Income: $2,062

2024 Dividend Income: $4,557

One of the few downsides here is that ETG's distributions aren't the most tax-efficient. The annual report indicates that a significant portion of the distributions are fueled by net investment income, which is classified as ordinary dividend income. Ordinary dividends are taxed at the highest rates, so a position in ETG may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account, such as an IRA.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on ETG due to the consistency around its performance, capital preservation, and dividend payouts. These traits make the fund an attractive choice for retired investors that are seeking stability in income and want a higher yield potential, while also preserving the initial invested capital. The fund provides a diverse exposure to some of the highest quality large-cap companies globally, while also demonstrating that its underlying strategy is effective over a long period of time through different market cycles.

data.title: Why The Iran Situation Is A Valuable Lesson
data.description:
By Samuel Rines

Geopolitical risk comes at you fast. But it also tends to fade quickly. Particularly, when those risks are not existential to the current economic environment. The initial reactions tend to be violent in their own right—from equities to commodities to currencies—and they do not tend to have staying power in a meaningful way. There is a difference between short-term shocks and long-tailed risks, and the recent events in the Middle East are an example of the first. The invasion of Ukraine is an example of the second. Most geopolitical events are headline-grabbing, not fundamental movers of the economic outlook.

Beginning decades ago, Iran was sanctioned out of the Western world. Iran is not integrated in a meaningful way to Western markets despite its oil wealth. Much of the roughly 1.5 million barrels of its exported oil goes to China, and a disruption in that supply could cause issues for that economy.

But a disruption in Iranian crude exports (even if taken to zero) would not be overly difficult to compensate for across markets.

During other periods of elevated geopolitical risk, oil was relatively scarce. There was little spare capacity. Fast-forward to the present, and U.S. shale has solved that problem. OPEC has plenty of spare capacity, amounting to about 2.5 times Iranian exports. Not to mention, Iran’s oil infrastructure has not been hit (at least, so far). Conspicuously, the U.S. position has typically been to implicitly support military action so long as energy infrastructure is not targeted.

If the oil side of the equation is largely moot, what exactly is the longer-term potential risk asset impact? The closure of the Strait of Hormuz comes up frequently as a potential retaliatory action. That is highly unlikely.

There are a number of reasons for this being an overblown threat. In no particular order:

  • Iran already at war with Israel. It does not want a Saudi problem too. It is worth remembering that Saudi Arabia and Iran are not friendly. In fact, the list of regional allies for Iran is rather short, as in non-existent.
  • It would predominately hurt China. China is the largest importer of oil through the Strait, and—given the current economic environment—would not be overly willing to “take the economic pain.”
  • Naval capabilities are not capable of doing so for any prolonged time period. Harassing boats? Yes. Closing the Strait? Probably not.

Simply, there is little to nothing Iran is capable of doing that would be broadly disruptive to markets for any notable period of time. Cyberattacks? Maybe. Kinetic retaliation? Likely, but limited. Shutting the Strait of Hormuz? Not going to happen. That leads to the conclusion it is most likely to be a short-term shock, not something more sinister.

Taken together, the market fading the risk makes sense. There are very few channels for the conflict to have a lasting negative effect on earnings or inflation. Elevated oil prices would create some inflation pressure in the U.S. (that might be why traders are pushing yields up a bit). But that would be transitory.

This is fundamentally different from the invasion of Ukraine. Russia was not an economically sanctioned outsider nor a country with little to no economic influence on the rest of the world. Europe was heavily reliant on Russian natural gas (cheap and pipeline-delivered). Following the invasion, Russia used that reliance as a tool to steer European policy and resolve around Ukraine.

That was effective until the U.S. (and others) replaced the supply. But that took the better part of 18 months, and the interim was highly disruptive to the European economy. Electricity prices spiked, causing consumers to feel pain and industry to throttle back.

Not to mention, coordinated sanctions and the advent of the “Price Cap Coalition” removed Russian oil exports from Western markets rapidly. Russia is not equivalent to Iran on the oil front, either. Russia produces roughly as much oil as Saudi Arabia, or three times that of Iran. It took time to reconfigure global energy markets both on the gas front and the oil front. Hence, the “long-tailed” reaction of risk asset markets.

The differences matter, and should not be ignored. The situation in the Middle East is far from ideal. But nothing really changes for the global economy as a result of the current developments. There will be saber rattling and threats. Commentators will continue to cite the Strait of Hormuz as a potential issue. That has never happened, and it is not going to this time around either. Simply, there are few options for Iran and none of them appear to be a meaningful threat to the global economy.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy”.

Original Post

data.title: U.S. Fed Keeps Interest Rates On Hold, Raises Inflation Outlook
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Reserve has kept interest rates on hold at 4.5% as policymakers keep an eye on sticky inflation levels. The central bank did say it still sees two more cuts this year. Sam Chai, Vice President, Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Anthony Okolie about the latest decision and the potential rate path ahead for the Fed.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: The US Federal Reserve has kept interest rates on hold, while also projecting higher inflation. But the central bank did indicate that it still sees two more rate cuts this year. Joining us now for more is Sam Chai of TD Asset Management.

And Sam, as I mentioned, the Fed held rates steady, as widely expected. Anything stand out for you today?

Sam Chai: So, as you mentioned, this is quite a widely expected decision. The Fed has held the rates unchanged, largely in light of the still very resilient hard data we've been seeing and also upside inflation risk, particularly from the trade policies. But coming to the meeting, the market had been a bit more focused on dot plots and any changes to economic projections. So let's dive a bit into that.

On the dot plot front, the Fed continued to forecast a two cuts baseline this year, so unchanged versus March. This is a bit of a dovish surprise because [the] market has been a bit torn whether we get one or two dots, dots like dot cuts, this year.

But for 2026, the Fed projects a one-cut baseline now. So down from two back in March. So that is a bit more of a hawkish surprise. So you see we get some dovishness and then some hawkishness in the dot forecast that way.

And when looking at the economic projections, expectation is that the revision will move towards a more stagflationary flavor. And that had been what we got actually. So the Fed had revised upwards inflation forecast and revised lower the GDP potential and the growth for this year and next year. So, indeed, the Fed does see more of a stagflationary outlook for the economy.

So overall, while we got some more new information from the Fed on their outlook, I don't think there's a lot of major surprises from what we're seeing here. The Fed has also, I think, succeeded in retaining a lot of optionality. By still signaling [a] two cut baseline this year, the Fed is implying that the coming meetings can still be live. And they can assess incoming data to potentially move nimbly as needed.

Anthony Okolie: OK. I want to go back to consumer price inflation because you mentioned that they've sort of upgraded their outlook there. But US inflation has surprised to the downside in recent months. And the job market is holding steady. What do you attribute this to? And is President Trump's call for a rate cut warranted?

Sam Chai: Yeah. I think it can be interesting to look at this question from a company-level perspective. So in general, I think many companies have prepared [for] the tariff impact well. Many companies were able to essentially delay in passing these price increases to consumers and basically absorb the margin impact on their own balance sheet in light of still-high uncertainty as to how the tariff policy will evolve in the coming weeks.

That being said, if you look at some of the corporate survey data, it does show that many firms are expecting to eventually pass these price increases through to consumers. So that does pose an upside risk to inflation going forward.

And also, if you look at the market-implied pricing of how CPI headline inflation year over year will be in the later half of this year, it does show market expectations that that will again exceed 3%. So if that is to materialize, that will be a meaningfully hawkish factor for the Fed as well.

So overall, I think in light of resilient hard data and upside inflation risks we just talked about, I think there's reasonable ground for the Fed to be patient here rather than delivering the 250 bps cut that President Trump is suggesting right now.

Anthony Okolie: OK. Let's talk about the bond market, because long-term US Treasury yields still remain elevated because of fiscal concerns, including the president's proposed tax bill. What does this mean for the Fed's path going forward?

Sam Chai: So, of course, the bill proposed had been a major debt sustainability concern. And just to quantify this a bit more, the version of the bill that's passed in the House essentially will result in a $3 trillion increase in deficit over the next 10 years.

So, for example, that would imply that the fiscal deficit as a percent of GDP in the US will increase from 6% this year to around 7% next year. So a very high number, considering where the economic cycle is right now.

And so, obviously, that has led to an increase in Treasury term premia. And expansionary fiscal policy itself is also reflationary. And it is reasonable to expect that the Fed will incorporate that into their baseline forecast and policy pass expectation going forward.

However, I think a more full incorporation of the fiscal impact will likely be reflected in the September meeting, when we have a much clearer view of what will actually be passed in that bill. Instead of having those fully reflected in the economic projection, we just see the Fed release.

Anthony Okolie: OK. Now, one of the things that had hit the headlines this year is the strength of the US dollar. And despite some short-term gains we have seen in the US dollar, it continues to come under pressure this year. Where do you see the currency going from here?

Sam Chai: So we talk about the concern on the US fiscal outlook front. Of course, there's also very high policy uncertainty on the US front as well. These factors are posing a threat to the long-term credibility and stability of the US dollar.

On the other hand, we have tailwinds for other major currencies. For example, the expected policy divergence between Bank of Japan and the Federal Reserve had been a tailwind for Japanese yen this year.

Another example is the Germany fiscal reform had been a supportive factor for long-term growth potential in Europe and has been supportive of euro. So all these macro factors are arguing for a medium-term depreciation of the dollar versus other currencies.

That being said, I think in the very near term there is, in my opinion, interesting risk-reward for a rebound in dollar strength in light of how much repricing the dollar had already done. So I think the answer to this question is more of a matter of the time frame we're talking about.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So given what's happened today, what are the indicators that you'll be watching closely over the next few weeks and months that could swing the Fed one way or another on rates?

Sam Chai: That would be labor market indicators. Of course, nonfarm payroll will be very critical to watch, but also more leading indicators, including jobless claims, or soft data, including PMI employment surveys, will be very important to watch and gauge how [the] labor market is evolving.

And the reason I emphasize these indicators is because I think that the bar to a bad news cut, meaning that labor market deteriorates and then Fed delivers cut, is that much lower than the bar for good news cut, meaning that the Fed cuts because of benign inflation.

Especially if we're talking about a scenario where the Fed delivers two or more cuts this year, I think that is quite a bit more likely the case that that is due to labor market deterioration versus we're just getting some benign inflation data.

Original Post
